coredata.dc:title: Flexible cesium lead halide CsPbX3@SiO2 (X = Cl, Br, I and their mixtures) perovskite nanocrystal films

Zhang, Canyun
Wang, Fengchao
Chen, Jin
Kong, Jinfang
Li, Lan
Xu, Jiayue
Zhang, Yan
coredata.dc:description: A centrifugally casting process was developed to prepare inorganic cesium lead halide CsPbX3 @SiO2 (X = Cl, Br, I and their mixtures) perovskite nanocrystal (PNC) films with high photoluminescence performance. By this costless solution-processing method, the obtained CsPbX3 @SiO2 nanocrystal (NC) films exhibit the strong emission covering the whole visible light range with high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of 94.0 %, 33.7 % and 10.9 % for the green, blue and red emission, respectively. By encapsulating CsPbX3 NCs with silica, the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films achieve remarkably enhanced stability against the ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, the thermal shock and the moist environment, especially for the CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films. Through using the flexible substrates such as polyethylene-terephthalate (PET), the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films show the excellent scalable and bendable capability. In addition, the LED devices assembled with the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films display a broader gamut and a highest color rendering index of 93.4. The achievement of the flexible CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films with high photoluminescence performance and better stability by the centrifugal method used here, opens a gate for practical applications of PNCs in the flexible luminescence and display devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photos for APTES-capped CsPbX3 (XCl, Br, I and their mixture) NC solution with different anion element composition in toluene under the ambient light (above) and UV lamp of 365 nm (below); (b) The corresponding PL spectra of APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution shown in (a); The TEM and HRTEM images for the as-grown CsPbBr3 NC solution (c) and the APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution with three typical anion element composition: CsPbBr3 (d), CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 (e) and CsPbBr1.5I1.5 (f), respectively.

(a) The image for CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films on PET substrates with an area of 1 × 1 cm2 under UV lamp; (b) PL spectra of CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films shown in (a); (c) Absorption spectra and (d) PL decay curves of CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the PNC films: (a) Cs 3d, (b) Pb 4 f and (c) Br 3d, respectively; (d) FTIR spectra of the PNC films.

The SEM photos of the PNC films: (a) CsPbBr3 films, (b) CsPbBr3 @SiO2 films, (c) CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 films and (d) CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 films, respectively. (e) Photo of a selected region of 40 µm× 40 µm area on CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films (the first picture on the left) and EDS mapping images of Cs, Pb, Br and Si elements from left to right.

Stability measurements of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films: (a) The remain PL intensity of CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films with time against the UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm); (b) The remain PL intensity of CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films in ten heating/cooling cycles; The PL stability of CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 NC films (c) and CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NC films (d) with time when they were irradiated by UV lamp or stored in the moist environment with relative humidity (RH) of 50 %.

(a) The gamut range of the blue, green and red CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films on the chromaticity diagram compared to the NTSC standard; (b) EL spectrum of the WLED device assembled through successively overlapping blue, green and red films on the 365 nm chip; (c) Photographs of the scalable and bendable light-emitting CsPbX3@SiO2 NC films deposited on flexible substrates with a larger area or textile fibers.

The fitting parameters of PL decay curves, the calculated average lifetimes (τave), PLQY, radiative transition recombination rate (KR), non-radiative transition recombination rate (KNR) and KR/KNR ratio for CsPbBr3 NC films and CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films.

head.ce:title: Flexible cesium lead halide CsPbX3@SiO2 (X = Cl, Br, I and their mixtures) perovskite nanocrystal films

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A centrifugally casting process was developed to prepare inorganic cesium lead halide CsPbX3 @SiO2 (X = Cl, Br, I and their mixtures) perovskite nanocrystal (PNC) films with high photoluminescence performance. By this costless solution-processing method, the obtained CsPbX3 @SiO2 nanocrystal (NC) films exhibit the strong emission covering the whole visible light range with high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of 94.0 %, 33.7 % and 10.9 % for the green, blue and red emission, respectively. By encapsulating CsPbX3 NCs with silica, the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films achieve remarkably enhanced stability against the ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, the thermal shock and the moist environment, especially for the CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films. Through using the flexible substrates such as polyethylene-terephthalate (PET), the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films show the excellent scalable and bendable capability. In addition, the LED devices assembled with the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films display a broader gamut and a highest color rendering index of 93.4. The achievement of the flexible CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films with high photoluminescence performance and better stability by the centrifugal method used here, opens a gate for practical applications of PNCs in the flexible luminescence and display devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic cesium lead halide CsPbX3 (X = Cl, Br, I and their mixtures) perovskite nanocrystals (PNCs) have blossomed into one of the most promising photoelectric materials in light-emitting and display devices, solar cells and photodetectors [1–9], due to the high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of over 90 %, the narrow PL full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 12–42 nm and the broad tunable optical band gap covering the whole visible wavelength range [10–14]. The synthesis by the traditional hot injection approach needs high-temperature reaction and inert gas protection [12], which lead to a relatively complicated operation process. In recent years, a supersaturated recrystallization method to prepare PNCs at room temperature with ligand-assisted reprecipitation, has attracted enormous attention owing to its features of facile synthesis and high PLQY (~95 %) [15]. Up to date, colloidal CsPbX3 nanocrystals (NCs) with high PLQY can be achieved by many preparation methods [16]. However, during the purification and application, it is difficult to preserve high luminescence performance of PNCs because of various damages, which lead to a sharp decline in luminescence [17,18]. On one hand, surface defects readily form due to ligand loss and ion migration [19,20], leading to high-density trapping states on the surface of NCs. On the other hand, based on their inherent ionic nature and low formation energy, PNCs are unstable under various environmental attacks such as thermal states, high humidity and ultraviolet light conditions [21–24]. Presently, the low-cost and high-efficiency synthetic processes are still lacking for preparing high-luminescence and stable PNC solid-state materials such as powders and films.

In order to improve the luminescence and stability of PNCs, many encapsulation strategies have been developed including the enhancement of ligand binding using more static coordination compounds [25–27], the encapsulation using zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) [28] or nanoscale alumina on CsPbX3 quantum dots (QDs) [26], the preparation of perovskite/polymer composites using polymeric substance [29,30] and so on. Although these studies have remarkably enhanced the luminescence and stability of PNCs, they still lead to different degrees of fluorescence quenching and have complex manufacture process [31,32].

Recently, a simple and costless solution chemical method was developed by our team to single-step synthesize large area of PNC films by centrifugally casting combining with the encapsulation of NCs with silica at room temperature, which avoids the damages in both PNC purification and film fabrication [33], compared with frequently-used spray-coating and spin-coating methods [34,35]. By this centrifugal method, PNCs can be uniformly coated on flexible substrates with scalable and bending properties, so that the application of CsPbX3 NCs can be extended to the field of flexible photoelectric devices [36]. Based on the rapid anion-exchange effect in halide perovskite materials due to the high anion diffusion mobility, the rigid nature of the cationic sublattice and the vacancy-assisted diffusion mechanism [37–39], the luminescence of PNC films covering the entire visible light range can be conveniently realized at room temperature.

In this work, we reported above achievements about the high-luminescence PNC films by this centrifugally casting method on flexible substrates. The obtained silica-coated CsPbX3 (X = Cl, Br, I and their mixtures) NC films possess excellent PLQY of 94.0 %, 33.7 % and 10.9 % for typical green, blue and red emission, respectively. The results demonstrate that the passivation of the silica precursor, i.e., APTES ligand to the surface of the CsPbX3 NCs is crucial to improve the radiation features of the PNC films, and simultaneously APTES also assists the PNC film formation on flexible organic substrates. The CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films also exhibit outstanding thermal and ultraviolet (UV) irradiation stability. Additionally, the LED devices fabricated using CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films show a broad gamut and a highest color rendering index of 93.4 under the excitation of a 365 nm UV lamp, which indicates the potential of PNC films here in the field of flexible photoelectric luminescence and display devices.

Lead bromide (PbBr2, 99 %), lead iodide (PbI2, 98 %), lead chloride (PbCl2, 99.5 %) and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, 99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Cesium bromide (CsBr, 99.5 %), toluene (99.5 %), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %), oleic acid (OA) and oleylamine (OLA, 80–90 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All regents were analytically pure grade and not repurified prior to being used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CsPbX3 precursor solution and APTES solution
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 2 ml OA and 0.3 ml OLA were mixed well in 20 ml DMF. 0.8 mmol CsBr and 0.8 mmol PbX2 (X = Cl, Br, I and their mixtures) were added into the above mixed solution under magnetic stirring for 15 min until the yellow precipitates were completely dissolved. Finally, the solution was laid aside for about 30 min to form the CsPbX3 precursor solution.

The preparation of APTES solution was accomplished by simple mixing and dilution. 1 mmol (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) was dissolved in 2 ml DMF and stirred well to form the APTES/DMF solution (0.5 mol/L). All above operations were completed at room temperature.

For synthesizing CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films, APTES solution of 80 μl was mixed well with the CsPbX3 precursor solution of 1 ml, corresponding to Si: Pb molar ratio of 1:1. Then above mixed solution of 100 μl was rapidly added into 5 ml toluene in a centrifugal tube to obtain the APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution. The PET substrate (1 ×1 cm2) was ultrasonically cleaned successively with acetone, alcohol and deionized water for 15 min and then dried in air. After putting the clean PET substrate into above NC solution, the centrifuge worked at 5000 rpm for 5 min and then the films were taken out of the solution and dried to obtain the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films. The entire synthesis process was completed at room temperature in air ambient. Upon preparing CsPbX3 NC films, the CsPbX3 precursor solution of 300 μl was directly added into 5 ml toluene in a centrifugal tube, and then the PET substrate was also put into the tube. Finally, a centrifugally casting process was performed with the speed rate of 8000 rpm for 5 min to obtain CsPbX3 NC films.

Herein, PL spectra of APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution and CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films were characterized by an FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK). An FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA) was used to examine the transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of as-grown and APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution. The absorption curves of CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films were measured under a UH4150 UV-Vis-near infrared spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The time-resolved PL spectra were performed with an FS5 fluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with a 450 nm excitation wavelength. The morphology and mapping images of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films were performed by an SEM 450/660 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (FEI, USA). An ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to measure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. The infrared spectra of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Bruker Dimension ICON atomic force microscope (AFM) was used to characterize the surface roughness of the PNC films. All of measurements were finished at room temperature.

Firstly, in this study, APTES-capped CsPbX3 (XCl, Br, I and their mixture) NCs in the solution used to prepare CsPbX3 @SiO2 films, were investigated through characterizing their PL features and crystalline structures, as shown in Fig. 1. Fig. 1(a) shows the photos for the APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution under the natural light (above) and 365 nm UV lamp (below), respectively. The solution exhibits different colors naturally and emits bright luminescence in the visible light range under UV lamp with the varying anion composition. In Fig. 1(b), the corresponding PL spectra of the colloidal PNC solution shown in Fig. 1(a) involve a broad wavelength range from 417 to 654 nm, which can be readily tuned by changing the halogen composition in PNCs. In the PL spectra, compared with the green emission (510 nm) of the APTES-capped CsPbBr3 NC solution, the PL emission of the APTES-capped CsPbX3 (XCl and Br mixture) NC solution gradually shifts toward the shorter wavelength direction with the increasing doping concentration of Cl anions. On the contrary, the PL peak of the APTES-capped CsPbX3 (X = Br and I mixture) NC solution moves to the longer wavelength side, when the proportion of I anions increases in halogen ions. As well known, the shift in PL peaks of PNCs can be supposed to result from the size-dependent effect controlled by the halide anion exchange reaction, meaning that the longer wavelength corresponds to the bigger crystalline size and vice versa [40–42]. In order to examine the micromorphology and crystallographic features of the APTES-capped CsPbX3 NCs, TEM and HRTEM measurements were performed, as shown in Fig. 1(d–f). For a comparison, the TEM image for CsPbBr3 NCs uncapped by APTES is also shown in Fig. 1(c), which exhibits a monodisperse square shape with a wide size distribution of 7–19 nm and an average size of 11.8 nm (Fig. S1a). In the top inset of Fig. 1(c), the CsPbBr3 NCs show a cubic structure with a spacing distance of 0.29 nm corresponding to the (200) crystal plane, which is consistent with the interplanar spacing of CsPbBr3 NCs prepared by the hot injection [43]. Compared with CsPbBr3 NCs without APTES-capping, the APTES-capped CsPbBr3 NCs in Fig. 1(d) display the similar shape and the smaller grain size ranging from 4 to 13 nm, with an average size of about 8.6 nm (Fig. S1b) and the same lattice distance of cubic phase in the top inset of Fig. 1(d). After capping by APTES, the reduced crystal size of CsPbBr3 NCs could result from a high steric hindrance of the branched APTES ligand coupled on the surface of PNCs. Based on related researches for enhancing luminescence performance and stability of PNCs by using APTES ligand [44,45], the high steric hindrance could prevent NCs from aggregating and thus effectively control the crystal size. In Fig. 1(e), the APTES-capped CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 NCs present a smaller size in the range of 3–11 nm with an average value of 6.8 nm (Fig. S1c), compared with that of the APTES-capped CsPbBr3 NCs in Fig. 1(d), due to the partial substitution of Br‾ anions by the smaller Cl‾ ions, although the cubic crystal structure and spacing index of NCs shown in the top inset of Fig. 1(e) remain almost constant. Noticeably, in Fig. 1(f), the APTES-capped CsPbBr1.5I1.5 NCs show many bigger rectangular particles in a length range of 18–28 nm with an average value of about 23.2 nm (Fig. S1d). As shown in the top inset of Fig. 1(f), the (200) lattice distance of APTES-capped CsPbBr1.5I1.5 NCs is determined to be 0.28 nm, which might result from a lattice contraction due to the increase of internal vacancy defects caused by the anion-exchange between Br‾ ions and I‾ ions [46].

Based on the preparation of the APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution, CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films were cast by a centrifugal method on the flexible PET substrates. Fig. S2 shows photos for three kinds of typical APTES-capped PNC solution, i.e., CsPbBr3, CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 and CsPbBr1.5I1.5 NCs, under UV light before and after centrifugation. From Fig. S2(a), (c) and (e), it can be seen that all APTES-capped PNCs uniformly disperse in solution prior to the centrifugation. After centrifugation, most of APTES-capped PNCs preferentially deposit on the surface of PET substrates, and only a small number of NCs with extremely small size still remain in supernatant, while few precipitates appear at the bottom of the centrifugal tube, as shown in Fig. S2(b), (d) and (f). The preferential precipitation of CsPbX3 NCs on PET materials found here could be attributed to the natural instincts of the APTES ligand. According to previous reports [33,47] and our research here, it is supposed that APTES, as an amino silane, contains two kinds of active groups such as -NH2 and SiOR- groups. The former could passivate the dangling bonds on the surface of PNCs through bonding with Pb2+ ions [48], like as an anchor on the surface of CsPbX3 NCs [49], beneficially improving the luminescence performance, and the latter might form a cross-linked SiO2 network encapsulation layer surrounding PNCs after hydrolysis, thus enhancing their stability [47,48]. In this study, the SiO2 encapsulation layers outside different PNCs could also connect together so as to form the NC films. On the other hand, as commonly-used coupling agents, APTES can readily adhere on the surface of organic or inorganic materials due to the presence of above two active groups. Besides APTES, another amino silane, 3-(Dimethoxymethylsilyl) propy lamine (C6H17NO2Si) was also used to prepare CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films, but its effect is worse than APTES, probably because different structures of two ligands result in different passivation and encapsulation capability. Additionally, several kinds of silane ligands without -NH2 group such as Tetramethoxysilane (C4H12O4Si), Triethoxymethylsilane (C7H18O3Si) and Hexadecyltrimethoxysilane (C19H42O3Si) were also attempted for the preparation of CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films. The results showed that very few NCs adhered to the substrates and distributed unevenly so that the PNC films couldn’t be attained, demonstrating that PNCs didn’t effectively accumulate onto the substrate surface after centrifugation, possibly resulting from no stronger chemical combination between ligands and PNCs due to the lack of -NH2 group in these ligands.

Fig. 2(a) displays photos for CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films on PET substrates obtained through centrifugating the APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution. Obviously, the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films also exhibit the bright luminescence across the visible range under UV light. The optical properties of CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films were systematically studied by PL emission spectra, optical absorption spectra and PL decay curves, as shown in Fig. 2(b), (c) and (d), respectively. Fig. 2(b) shows the corresponding PL wavelength range of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films from 425 to 630 nm, which is a bit narrower than that of the APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution in Fig. 1(b), because the emission peak of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films prepared by the centrifugal method has a red shift compared with that of the corresponding APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution. For the APTES-capped CsPbX3 NC solution with a higher I/Br ratio, the obtained CsPbX3 @SiO2 films have very weak luminescence, thus the spectral range of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films slightly narrows down. This phenomenon about the red shift of the emission peak observed is a common occurrence during precipitation for the preparation of PNC films from the solution phase [17], which primarily results from the size selectivity of NCs from the solution to the solid phase during centrifugation. On the other hand, the aggregation effect of PNCs could also cause the red shift of the emission wavelength owing to the size increase, particularly for the Br/I mixed CsPbX3 @SiO2 NCs, because the completed encapsulation of the NCs by APTES might be difficult due to their bigger size shown in Fig. 1(f). Fig. 2(c) shows the optical steady-state absorption spectra of several CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films with different halogen composition such as Br:Cl= 1.5:1.5, Br:Cl= 2:1, pure Br, Br:I= 2:1 and Br:I= 1.5:1.5 corresponding to the film colors from blue to red. From Fig. 2(c), it can be seen that above CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films show the obvious optical absorption edges near 455, 492, 520, 554 and 617 nm, respectively, which exhibit a gradual red-shift trend with above halogen composition variation, well coinciding with the shift of PL emission in Fig. 2(b).

As the representative samples, three kinds of typical PNC films, i.e., CsPbBr3 @SiO2, CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 and CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 films are selected to evaluate their PLQYs, which are 94.0 %, 33.7 % and 10.9 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. S3(b), (c) and (d). Additionally, PLQY of CsPbBr3 NC films without the SiO2 encapsulation was also measured to be 14.2 % shown in Fig. S3(a) for comparison purpose. The remarkable improvement in PLQY of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films compared with that of the as-grown CsPbBr3 NC films demonstrates the positive role of SiO2 encapsulation in enhancing PL emission performance of PNCs. In order to understand the origin of high PLQY of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films, the UV–visible absorption and PL emission spectra of the as-grown CsPbBr3 NC films were also assessed to contrast them with the optical absorption and emission features of the CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films, as shown in Fig. S4. In Fig. S4(a), the absorption curve and PL spectrum of the as-grown CsPbBr3 NC films show a moderate band edge near 525 nm and a longer emission wavelength of 520 nm, respectively, compared with a steep absorption edge near 520 nm and a 518 nm PL wavelength for CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films in Fig. S4(b). The blue shift of the band edge and emission peak for CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films compared with that of unencapsulated CsPbBr3 NC films results from the broadening effect of the band gap due to the reduced size of NCs encapsulated by SiO2, which is consistent with the size change of NCs in solution in Fig. 1(c) and (d). Noticeably, the absorption spectrum of CsPbBr3 NC films obviously exhibits a long tail in the wavelength range from 525 to 600 nm, which could be related to the band edge state of higher density caused by surface defects of NCs before encapsulating with SiO2 [50]. After encapsulation, the sharp absorption edge and the unobservable band tail of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films implies the great reduction of defect states, which is beneficial to improve the luminous performance of PNC films. Additionally, both CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films show a small Stokes shift of about 15 nm between the absorption and PL emission spectra, indicating that PL emission are mainly related with the direct transition recombination [40,51]. In this study, PLQY of CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 NC films was measured to be 33.7 %, while PLQY is only 10.9 % for CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NC films, which are much lower than that of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films. The sharply reduced PLQYs of the mixed-halide CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films could mainly result from a large amount of internal defects generated during the lattice reconstruction of PNCs [52].

For the purpose of examining the effectiveness of the centrifugal film-casting technology in maintaining the luminescence performance of PNCs, PLQYs of the NC solution before centrifugating were also determined to be 43.0 %, 98.0 %, 44.5 % and 35.3 % for the as-grown CsPbBr3 NC solution, the APTES-capped CsPbBr3, CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 and CsPbBr1.5I1.5 NC solution, respectively. The largely reduced PLQY of the as-grown CsPbBr3 NC films than that of its solution phase is supposed to be caused by the increase of surface defects of PNCs, as a result of the desorption of OA and OLA ligands from the surface of NCs during the centrifugation, due to the weaker combination between these ligands and PNCs. It is worth noting that PLQY of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films is extremely close to that of its solution, demonstrating the capability of the centrifugally casting technology combining with the SiO2 encapsulation used here in preserving the luminescence features of PNCs. For the mixed-halide CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 NC films, they can inherit a large part of PLQY of the solution phase, while PLQY of the CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NC films drops down to one third of the PLQY value of the solution. The difficulty in maintaining the luminescence of the CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NCs is possibly related with the lower effectiveness of the SiO2 encapsulation due to the larger crystal grains and the rectangular shape of CsPbBr1.5I1.5 NCs, as shown in Fig. 1(f).

For further exploring the internal luminescence mechanism of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films, their time-resolved PL decay curves were analyzed in detail, as shown in Fig. 2(d). According to a biexponential function, the PL decay curves can be fitted well, and all fitting parameters are shown in Table 1, where A1 and A2 are weights of multiple exponential functions, τ1 and τ2 represent time constants for short-lived and long-lived lifetimes related to the excitonic state emission and trapping state emission [53], respectively.

In Table 1, the average lifetimes (τave) of two PL emissions of all PNC films were calculated according to Eq.1 [53]. Additionally, the radiative transition rate (KR), the non-radiative transition rate (KNR) and the KR/KNR ratio were also estimated on the basis of Eqs.2, 3, 4 and 5 [48,54], where Φ stands for PLQY. For CsPbBr3 NC films, the probability of the non-radiative transition is 6 times higher than that of the radiative transition, probably resulting from surface defects of high density, which would readily form the trap centers for the photo-induced carriers and thus substantially reduce the radiation recombination rate, leading to the lower PLQY. Compared with CsPbBr3 NC films, after modification with the terminated ligands, the KR value of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films is 16 times higher than that of KNR instead, which demonstrates the effective passivation of APTES ligand to surface defects of PNCs, leading to a significant increase of PLQY from 14.2 % to 94.0 %. As regards to the mixed-halogen CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 NC films, the lower radiation transition rate would result from the high-density interior structural defects caused by lattice mismatching during the anion-exchange process. For the CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NC films, the low KR/KNR ratio close to that of the CsPbBr3 NC films could be caused not only by the internal structural defects, but also by the surface defects as a result of the lower ligand encapsulation effect, which is consistent with the lowest PLQY of CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NC films.

Fig. 3 shows XPS and FTIR spectra of four typical NC film samples, i.e., CsPbBr3 films, CsPbBr3 @SiO2 films, CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 films and CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 films. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Cs 3d, Pb 4 f and Br 3d for these films were shown in Fig. 3(a), (b) and (c), respectively. In Fig. 3(a), for CsPbBr3 @SiO2 films, CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 films and CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 films, it can be seen that the core level of Cs 3d has no significant change compared with that of the CsPbBr3 films. However, the Pb 4 f core levels of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 films and CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 films obviously move toward the higher energy side compared with that of CsPbBr3 films, while the Pb 4 f core level stays still for CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 films. The increased binding energy of Pb2+ ions could be due to the strong combination of the -NH2 group in the APTES molecule and the unsaturated Pb ions on the surface of PNCs to form a stable structure [55]. The unmovable Pb 4 f core level of CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 films suggests that it is difficult to encapsulate CsPbBr1.5I1.5 NCs with APTES ligand because of the bigger crystal grains, which coincides with TEM results in Fig. 1(f) and poor PL features mentioned above. In Fig. 3(c), the high energy shoulder of Br 3d peak of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films indicates the abundant Br ions on the surface of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NCs [15]. According to the EDS results (Fig. S5), the Br/Pb atomic ratio has an enhancement from 3.2:1 of CsPbBr3 NC films to 4.2:1 of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films, confirming the XPS result of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films. The enrichment of halide ions on the surface of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NCs would also inhibit the capture of the excited carriers and improve PLQY of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films [15]. Additionally, the XPS survey spectra of the PNC films shown in Fig. S6 affirm the presence of Cl, I, N, O and Si elements. In Fig. S6 (a), the weak peak of N1s located at 401.1 eV results from the protonylated -NH3 group of the OLA ligand in CsPbBr3 NC films [42]. After the capping modification by the APTES ligand, the peak intensity of N 1 s has a significant enhancement in Fig. S6(b), (c) and (d), which implies that the -NH2 group of the APTES molecule binds on the surface of the PNCs [56]. In Fig. S6(b), (c) and (d), the enhanced O1s peak at 531.7 eV and Si 2p peak at 101.8 eV verify the formation of SiO2 after the hydrolysis of the APTES ligand [57,58]. FTIR spectra were measured to detect the surface functional groups on the surface of PNCs, as shown in Fig. 3(d). For CsPbBr3 films, a weak peak in the broad range of 3080–3700 cm−1 originates from the stretching vibration of -OH group in OLA ligand [59], while a strong asymmetric peak of CsPbX3 @SiO2 films at 3450 cm−1 results from -OH group generated after the hydrolysis of APTES [48,60]. Meanwhile, compared with FTIR spectrum of CsPbBr3 films, the additional Si-O-Si peaks at 1120 cm−1, 1030 cm−1 and 793 cm−1 indicate the formation of silica reticular structure in CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films [47,61].

In order to examine the morphological features of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films, SEM, EDS and AFM characterizations were performed to measure surface morphology, the status of element distribution and roughness, as shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S7. Fig. 4(a) shows many bigger particles with the inhomogeneous size across the surface of the CsPbBr3 NC films, because the desorption of OA and OLA ligands from the surface of CsPbBr3 NCs during the centrifugation would easily result in the aggregation effect of PNCs. Noticeably, the CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films shown in Fig. 4(b) exhibit a rather uniform surface, where CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NCs are smaller and more homogeneous in the size distribution than CsPbBr3 NCs. On the surface of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films, we randomly selected a region of 40 µm× 40 µm area, as shown in the first picture on the left in Fig. 4(e), for the scanning electron microscopy-energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDS) elemental mapping measurements. From left to right, the mapping images of Cs, Pb, Br and Si elements indicate that they are uniformly distributed in the PNC films. Fig. 4(c) shows that the CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 NC films also have a uniform surface due to the small size of CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 NCs, while CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NC films display the bigger particle size and an uneven surface, as shown in Fig. 4(d). The surface root-mean-square roughness of all PNC films was further studied by using the 3D atomic force microscopy (AFM), as shown in Fig. S7, which indicates the roughness values are 51.6 nm, 15.5 nm, 25.6 nm and 31.9 nm for CsPbBr3 NC films, CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films, CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 NC films and CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NC films, respectively. It can be evidently found that the surface smoothness of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films is improved compared with that of CsPbBr3 NC films. This smoother surface could reduce the loss of the pumping light incident from the contact surface between the air and the PNC films to a certain degree and thus increase the pumping light propagating in the PNC films [62], which may be conducive to the increased PLQY of the PNC films, especially for CsPbBr3 @SiO2 and CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 NC films. However, the lower PLQY of CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NC films could suffer from the bigger grain size and high-density structural defects although the surface smoothness has been improved.

For the sake of practical applications, in this study, the stability of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films was investigated by analyzing the effects of UV lamp irradiation, heating and moist conditions on optical properties of the PNC films, as shown in Fig. 5. Fig. 5(a) shows the variety of PL intensity of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films and CsPbBr3 NC films with time within 60 days under a 365 nm UV light in a darkroom in air ambient. It can be seen that the PL intensity of the CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films still preserves 98 % of the original intensity after 60 days, while the PL intensity of CsPbBr3 NC films drops sharply and only remains 24 % of the initial value after the same time. Meanwhile, the PL peak position of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films changes a little with time within 60 days, as shown in Fig. S8(a). In addition, the CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films exhibit the higher stability against thermal attack. Fig. S8(b) shows the detailed variety curves of PL intensity of CsPbBr3 NC films and CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films during 10 consecutive heating/cooling cycles from 20 to 100 ℃ and then to 20 ℃. Fig. 5(b) shows remain PL intensity of CsPbBr3 NC films and CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films tested after every heating/cooling process. It can be observed that the PL intensity of two kinds of PNC films decreases with the increased heating/cooling cycle times. After ten cycles of heating/cooling, the final PL intensity of the CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films still maintains 80 % of its initial intensity, while the CsPbBr3 NC films only holds 22 % of its initial data. However, the PL intensity of both films is unrecoverable, which means that the nonradiative recombination centers are thermally activated during the thermal cycles, leading to the degradation of PL performance. The stability data of CsPbBr3 NC films and CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films stored in the moist environment with relative humidity (RH) of 50 % are shown in Fig. S9 in Supporting Information. It can be seen that the PL intensity of the CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films can maintain 82 % after 28 days of storage, while the unwrapped CsPbBr3 NC films only maintain 5.6 % of the original luminescence performance after 28 days. It is worth acknowledging that the statistically enhanced irradiation, thermal and moist stability of CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films can be attributed to the protection of the formed SiO2 structure, as reported in many recent studies [45]. The stability of the mixed-halide CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films was also studied, as shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d). In Fig. 5(c), the PL emission of CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 @SiO2 NC films presents a gradual decrease trend and the finial PL intensity falls down to 14 % of the initial data under UV irradiation within four weeks, while their PL intensity remains 21 % under the moisture condition with RH ≈ 50 % after 28 days. As for CsPbBr1.5I1.5 @SiO2 NC films, their PL intensity can remain 25 % and 34 % under UV irradiation and the humid environment, respectively. The poor environmental stability of PL emission of mixed-halide CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films fundamentally suffers from more internal and surface defects and lower formation energy [63]. The luminescent properties and stability of the mixed-halogen PNC films could be improved through doping other ions inside the lattice, encapsulating with multiple ligands, treating with a nickel bromide (NiBr2) or CdX2 solution as reported in recent researches [64,65], or combining with the advantages of other synthesis methods [66,67], which will be further studied in the future.

In order to evaluate the potential application of CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films in the light-emitting and display field, their photochromic properties were measured and several simple applications were attempted, as shown in Fig. 6. Fig. 6(a) shows a gamut of the blue, green and red CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films as wide as 126.4 % of the color space of National Television Systems Committee (NTSC) TV color standard, when these films are excited by a naked blue chip (365 nm). In addition, a WLED device was assembled through successively overlapping blue, green and red films on the 365 nm chip, as shown in the top inset of Fig. 6(b). The obtained LED working at a 30 mA current achieves excellent photochromic parameters shown in Fig. 6(b), i.e., a highest Color Rendering Index (CRI) of 93.4 and a Commission International de I′ Eclairage (CIE) color coordinate of (0.327, 0.343), which provides the possibility to get the high CRI white light devices in the photoelectric display field. Moreover, the luminous efficiency of 47.75 lm/W is achieved for the obtained WLED. In addition, for further evaluating the working stability of the WLED, Fig. S10 plots the EL spectra of WLED before/after continuous working for 36 h under an operating current of 3 mA. The results show that the EL spectra can remain stable even after continuous work for 36 h. Furthermore, the excitation stability can be improved in the future by better designing the device structure with a longer excitation distance from the light source to the PNC films. Fig. 6(c) shows the excellent scalable and bendable capability of the light-emitting CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films on flexible substrates with a larger area. It is interesting that the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NCs can homogeneously adhere to the surface of the textile fibers by this centrifugal method used here, meaning the possible applications in the wearable technology.

In summary, we synthesized the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films with high PLQY and better stability by a facile centrifugally casting process at room temperature. The photoluminescence of the obtained CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films almost covers the whole visible light range, and the PLQYs of the typical green, blue and red CsPbX3 @SiO2 films can reach up to 94.0 %, 33.7 % and 10.9 %, respectively. The experimental facts demonstrate that the passivation of APTES ligand to the surface of the CsPbX3 NCs is crucial to improve the radiation features of the perovskite NC films. The stability measurements indicate that the capacity of the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films against various environmental factors such as the irradiation, the thermal attack and the humidity, is remarkably enhanced by means of the encapsulation of CsPbX3 NCs with silica in the films, particularly for the CsPbBr3 @SiO2 NC films. In terms of the application potentials, the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films exhibit the excellent scalable and bendable capability by using polyethylene-terephthalate (PET) as the flexible substrates. Additionally, the LED devices assembled with the CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films show the better photochromic properties such as the broader gamut and higher color rendering index of 93.4. The study about the flexible CsPbX3 @SiO2 NC films with high photoluminescence performance and better stability by this simple solution-processing method, provides an opportunity for promoting practical applications of CsPbX3 NC materials in the luminescence and display fields.
